column1: Overview of the three major platforms for XR development and their key features.

column4: Web API for using XR devices in a web browser; predominantly open-source

column2: Cross-platform, rapid prototyping, mobile/stand-alone VR, big community and learning resources
column3: High-fidelity graphics, photorealism, complex physics, cinematic quality, no programming experience needed (Blueprints)
column4: Accessibility, instant access via web browser, platform-agnostic, low barrier to entry, no installation needed for end user

column2: Interactive training, medical simulations, mobile AR/VR field studies, UI-heavy apps
column3: High-resolution scientific visualization, complex real-time physical simulations, architectural/engineering visualization
column4: Dissemination of results, online education, lightweight data visualization, immersive analytics on the web

column2: Very good, but often requires more configuration to match Unreal Engine's out-of-the-box quality
column3: Industry-leading for photorealistic and high-end rendering
column4: Dependent on the underlying library and browser capabilities; typically lower than native game engines

column2: Generally well-optimized for a broad range of hardware, better for mobile/lower-end devices
column3: Requires significantly higher-end hardware (GPU/RAM) to leverage full graphical features, default settings need much compute
column4: Lower performance ceiling than native game engines due to browser overhead, but often sufficient for many data visualization tasks

column2: Builds for almost all environments (desktop, mobile, stand-alone VR, WebXR), also gaming consoles
column3: Excels on PC VR and high-end consoles; limited official WebXR support
column4: Highest accessibility; no installation required; runs on any WebXR-compatible browser/device
